questions.question: Explain the Many-to-One multithreading model and state one major disadvantage.
keyPoints: Maps multiple user threads to a single kernel thread
All thread management done in user space by thread library
Disadvantage: If one thread blocks on I/O, entire process blocks
No true parallelism on multiprocessor systems

questions.question: What is thread pooling? Explain any two advantages of using a thread pool.
keyPoints: Thread pool is a collection of pre-created reusable threads
Advantage 1: Reduces overhead of creating new threads for each task
Advantage 2: Improves response time for short-lived tasks
Advantage 3: Limits number of concurrent threads

questions.question: Differenti between user-level thread scheduling and kernel-level thread scheduling.
keyPoints: User-level: Thread library handles scheduling, no kernel involvement, faster context switches
Kernel-level: OS kernel schedules threads directly, true parallelism possible
User-level: One thread blocking blocks entire process in Many-to-One model
Kernel-level: One thread blocking does not affect others in One-to-One model

questions.question: Explain the One-to-One multithreading model and mention its limitations.
keyPoints: Each user thread maps to exactly one kernel thread
Provides true parallelism on multiprocessor systems
Used in Windows and modern Java JVM
Limitation: Number of kernel threads limited by system resources
Creates more overhead due to kernel thread creation

questions.question: What is the Many-to-Many multithreading model? How does it overcome the limitations of Many-to-One model?
keyPoints: Many user threads multiplexed to many (or fewer) kernel threads
Combines advantages of both models
Overcomes Many-to-One limitation: kernel can schedule other threads when one blocks
Allows true parallelism on multiprocessor systems
Number of kernel threads can be optimized for application needs

questions.question: Explain the concept of green threads. Why are they not used in modern systems?
keyPoints: Green threads are user-level threads simulated by runtime, not OS kernel
Used in early Java versions (pre-Java 1.2)
Cannot take advantage of multiple processors
If one green thread blocks (e.g., I/O), all green threads block
Modern systems use native threads (One-to-One model) for true parallelism

questions.question: Define thread concurrency and thread parallelism. Can you have concurrency without parallelism?
keyPoints: Concurrency: Multiple threads make progress over overlapping time periods
Parallelism: Multiple threads execute simultaneously on multiple CPUs
Yes, concurrency can exist without parallelism on single-CPU systems
Threads take turns (time-sliced) creating interleaved execution
Parallelism requires multiple processing units

questions.question: Describe the Three multithreading models (Many-to-One, One-to-One, and Many-to-Many) with diagrams. Compare their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Many-to-One: Multiple user threads → One kernel thread - simple but blocks entire process on I/O
One-to-One: One user thread → One kernel thread - true parallelism but limited by kernel thread count
Many-to-Many: Multiple user threads → Multiple kernel threads - combines benefits, complex implementation
Comparison table covering: parallelism, blocking behavior, implementation complexity, scalability
Real-world examples: Solaris (Many-to-Many), Windows (One-to-One), early Java (Many-to-One/Green threads)

questions.question: Explain the Two-Level multithreading model. How does it differ from the Many-to-Many model? Why might an operating system choose to implement this model?
keyPoints: Two-Level: Hybrid model with some user threads bound to dedicated kernel threads, others multiplexed
Difference from Many-to-Many: Bound threads get guaranteed CPU time without competition
Why use it: Provides guaranteed real-time or high-priority thread execution
Allows flexible resource allocation for different thread priorities
Example: Used in IRIX and some Solaris configurations
Drawback: More complex than pure Many-to-Many, can lead to starvation of unbound threads

questions.question: Write and explain a C program using Pthreads to create a thread pool. Include the main function, worker thread function, and task queue management.
keyPoints: Thread pool structure with array of threads and task queue
Worker function that continuously waits for tasks from queue
Main function creates pool of threads at startup
Task submission function adds work to queue and signals worker
Proper mutex locks for queue synchronization
Condition variables for thread communication
Cleanup and thread termination handling
Shows practical implementation of thread pooling concept

questions.question: Discuss the various threading issues that must be considered in multithreaded programs: signal handling, thread cancellation, thread creation, and thread termination.
keyPoints: Signal Handling: Which thread should handle signals? Options include synchronous/async delivery, signal mask inheritance
Thread Cancellation: Thread termination before completion - two types: deferred (checks cancellation point) and asynchronous
Thread Creation: Overhead of creating threads vs benefits of parallelism, thread attributes configuration
Thread Termination: Resource cleanup, joining threads, detached threads, preventing zombie threads
Additional issues: Thread-local storage, scheduling, priority, synchronization overhead
